summary: I was born and raised in Santa Rosa, California, where I attended school and developed an early interest in art. It was my hometown and the place where my artistic journey began before I moved on to study in Berkeley. Santa Rosa holds a special place in my heart as the foundation of my education and childhood.

During my high school years, I studied at the Arts & Crafts school in Berkeley, California. I continued part-time studies there while working as a commercial artist. Berkeley was an important place for my artistic development and education during my youth.

San Francisco was the heart of my adult life and art career, where I met Otis Oldfield and became deeply involved in the local art scene. We lived there with our two daughters and connected with many artists and institutions. The city was vibrant with creativity, and it was where Otis taught and exhibited his work.

Otis Oldfield was my husband and a central figure in my life and art world. He studied extensively in Paris from 1909 to 1924, which shaped his unique style. Otis taught at the Mark Hopkins Art Institute, illustrated books, and was deeply involved in the San Francisco art community. He was dedicated to teaching and influenced many students, including Nathan Oliveira and Richard Diebenkorn.

I took night classes at the Mark Hopkins Art Institute in San Francisco, where Otis Oldfield taught private classes. It was through this institute that Otis and I met and became close. The school played a significant role in our artistic lives and connections.

Otis spent many years in Paris from 1909 to 1924 studying and developing his art. His time there greatly influenced his style, especially his strong drawing skills and appreciation for artists like Renoir and Matisse. Paris was a formative place for Otis before he returned to San Francisco.

Otis did not follow the Abstract Expressionism trend of the 1950s, choosing instead to remain true to his individual style focused on nature and decorative art. He was critical of avant-garde movements and preferred more traditional influences. This stance set him apart from many contemporary artists of his time.

Otis admired Pierre-Auguste Renoir and valued his work highly. Renoir's influence was part of Otis's preference for nature and decorative art rather than avant-garde styles. This admiration shaped Otis's artistic philosophy and teaching.

Henri Matisse was another artist Otis admired and looked up to. His appreciation for Matisse reflected his commitment to decorative art and strong drawing skills. This admiration influenced Otis's work and teaching approach.

Rinaldo Cuneo was a close friend and fellow artist in the San Francisco art community. Otis and I often socialized with him and other artists at local spots like Elgin's and Coppa's. Cuneo also collaborated with Otis on the Coit Tower murals, making him an important figure in our artistic circle.

Ralph Stackpole was a dear friend and part of our vibrant San Francisco art community. Diego Rivera even lived in Stackpole's studio for a time, which connected us all. We shared many experiences and supported each other in our artistic endeavors.

Maynard Dixon was among the artists we were close to in San Francisco. We often gathered together in social settings, fostering a strong and supportive art community. His presence enriched our artistic lives during that period.

During World War II, Otis and I both volunteered to help rehabilitate wounded veterans through art. Otis worked with the Arts & Crafts school, while I volunteered with the Red Cross Bureau of Inquiry. We also juried artwork created by hospitalized veterans, contributing to their recovery and morale.

I volunteered with the Red Cross Bureau of Inquiry during World War II, assisting in the rehabilitation of wounded veterans through art. This work was deeply meaningful and complemented Otis's efforts at the Arts & Crafts school. The Red Cross provided a vital platform for supporting veterans during the war.

Diego Rivera was a kind and sweet man who visited San Francisco and was part of our art circle. He lived for a time in Ralph Stackpole's studio and influenced many local mural artists. We also knew Frida Kahlo and witnessed the impact Diego had on the community.

We knew Frida Kahlo through her connection with Diego Rivera during their time in San Francisco. Her presence was part of the vibrant artistic environment that influenced many local artists. Frida's relationship with Diego added depth to the cultural scene we experienced.

Otis, along with Rinaldo Cuneo and others, painted murals for Coit Tower, which was an exciting and historic project. Watching the artists at work there was inspiring, and it was a significant moment in our lives. Coit Tower remains a landmark of San Francisco's artistic heritage.

Otis joined Kenneth Rexroth's artists group briefly, though he was reluctant to be part of any group. Rexroth called Otis an anarchist, a label Otis agreed with, but he didn't stay long. This group was one of the few artist collectives Otis associated with during his career.

Otis taught many students, including Nathan Oliveira, who went on to become a significant artist. His teaching was influential and focused on strong drawing skills and individual style. Nathan was one of the many artists shaped by Otis's dedication and guidance.

Richard Diebenkorn was among Otis's students and benefited greatly from his teaching. Otis's emphasis on drawing and his individual artistic philosophy helped shape Diebenkorn's career. Their relationship was an important part of the San Francisco art education scene.

Hassel Smith was another student of Otis who was influenced by his teaching. Otis's dedication to art education and his unique style left a lasting impact on Smith and many others. This mentorship was a key part of Otis's legacy.
